title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Turkey — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Turkish Coffee
items.text: Finely ground coffee brewed in a small copper pot (cezve) and served in tiny cups, often with cardamom. It's thick, strong, and meant to be sipped slowly while the grounds settle at the bottom—don't drink the sludge.

items.label: Order by sweetness level
items.text: Ask for şekersiz (no sugar), az şekerli (little sugar), orta şekerli (medium), or çok şekerli (very sweet). The coffee is prepared individually in the pot, so specify before brewing.

items.label: Traditional kahvehane
items.text: These male-dominated coffee houses serve strong coffee, tea, and water pipe (nargile) in a social setting. They're authentic but can feel unwelcoming to solo female travelers; mixed cafés are more inclusive.

items.label: Skip the tourist café chains
items.text: Avoid Starbucks-style places in major squares—local neighborhood cafés serve better quality Turkish coffee and are cheaper. Ask your hotel for recommendations in residential areas.

items.label: Meze
items.text: Small appetizers and dips served as starters: hummus, baba ganoush, dolma (stuffed grape leaves), cheese, olives, and seasonal vegetables. Order several to share and enjoy the grazing tradition.

items.label: Kebab varieties
items.text: Döner (meat rotating on a spit), Adana (spiced ground meat grilled on skewers), and Doner Iskender (döner over bread with yogurt and tomato sauce) are regional standards. Each region claims superiority—try multiple versions.

items.label: Pide
items.text: A boat-shaped flatbread topped with cheese, meat, or egg and baked until crispy. It's lighter than pizza and often better value for money than kebabs.

items.label: Lahmacun
items.text: A thin, crispy flatbread topped with minced meat and herbs, often called 'Turkish pizza.' Roll it up with lemon and parsley for the authentic experience.

items.label: Menemen
items.text: A breakfast or lunch scrambled egg dish with tomatoes, peppers, and onions, often topped with cheese. It's simple, cheap, and found everywhere.

items.label: Manti
items.text: Tiny pasta parcels filled with meat or potato, topped with yogurt and spiced tomato sauce. Order at lunch in local restaurants rather than tourist spots for better quality.

items.label: Ask for the daily special (günün yemeği)
items.text: Restaurants always have a dish cooked fresh that day, usually cheaper and better quality than standard menu items. It changes daily and isn't always written down.

items.label: Meze comes first, then ask for mains
items.text: Order small plates to start while deciding on kebabs or grilled fish. The waiter won't bring main courses until you ask, avoiding rushed meals.

items.label: Watch locals or point at plates
items.text: If the menu is confusing, point to dishes at nearby tables or ask the waiter to show you what's available in the kitchen. Many restaurants have no English menu but cooks speak visual.

items.label: Bread and water arrive automatically
items.text: You'll be charged for bread (ekmek) and bottled water unless you explicitly refuse at the start. Tap water is safe to drink and free if requested.

items.label: Avoid restaurants with picture menus near major sites
items.text: These are almost always tourist traps with inflated prices and mediocre food. Walk 5-10 minutes away from famous landmarks to find authentic, cheaper places.

items.label: Kebab house (kebapçı)
items.text: Casual standing-room or basic-seating spots serving grilled meats, pide, and salads. Cheap, fast, and usually excellent quality—locals eat here daily.

items.label: Balık restaurant (seafood by the Bosphorus or Aegean)
items.text: Order fresh fish by weight (usually priced per kilo), grilled simply with lemon and olive oil. Expect higher prices near tourist zones; ask locals for neighborhood spots.

items.label: Breakfast spot (kahvaltı)
items.text: Turkish breakfast is a leisurely spread of breads, cheeses, olives, tomatoes, cucumbers, eggs, and honey. Go mid-morning to a neighborhood place for an affordable feast.

items.label: Meyhane (tavern or wine bar)
items.text: Casual dining with meze, grilled items, and local wine or rakı. These are social, lively spaces, perfect for evening meals and people-watching.

items.label: Eat where construction workers eat
items.text: Look for humble spots packed with locals during lunch (noon to 2pm). Quality is high, prices are low, and you'll never feel like a tourist.

items.label: Rakı (anise-flavored spirit)
items.text: Turkey's national drink, 40% alcohol, traditionally diluted with water (turns milky white, called 'lion's milk'). Sipped slowly with meze as a social drink, not a shot.

items.label: Turkish wine
items.text: Regions like Cappadocia and the Aegean coast produce good-quality reds and whites at reasonable prices. Kavaklidere and Doluca are reliable supermarket brands; local wineries offer tastings.

items.label: Ayran
items.text: A salty yogurt drink served ice-cold, perfect for hot days and pairs well with kebabs. It's non-alcoholic, refreshing, and costs under a dollar.

items.label: Turkish tea (çay)
items.text: Black tea served in small glasses with a saucer, often with apple or lemon flavors available. It's cheaper than coffee and endlessly refilled at restaurants without extra charge.

items.label: Alcohol is less visible in conservative areas
items.text: In Istanbul and coastal tourist zones, wine and beer are readily available. In inland or religious neighborhoods, options may be limited; buy ahead if needed.

items.label: Tipping is expected but modest
items.text: Leave 5-10% of the bill or round up to the nearest convenient number. High-end restaurants expect 10-15%; street food doesn't require tips.

items.label: Meals are social and unhurried
items.text: Turks linger over food and conversation. The meal pace is slow, and rushing is considered rude. Restaurants won't bring the bill until you ask (hesap, lütfen).

items.label: Remove shoes when entering traditional settings
items.text: Some meyhanes or family-run restaurants have low seating with cushions—remove shoes before stepping up onto the platform.

items.label: Refuse hospitality politely but briefly
items.text: Hosts (especially in small towns) may offer tea or coffee. Refusing once is expected; accepting on the second or third offer shows respect. A simple 'teşekkür ederim' (thank you) goes far.

items.label: Use bread as a utensil
items.text: Tear off pieces of bread to scoop up food, hummus, or sauce. Eating with your right hand is customary and considered more polite.

items.label: Cash is still king; card acceptance varies
items.text: Many small restaurants and kebab shops are cash-only. ATMs are plentiful in cities, but carry cash when visiting rural or less touristy areas.

items.label: Ask for prices upfront at fish restaurants
items.text: Fish is priced by weight and can be expensive if you're unaware. Confirm the total cost before ordering or request a specific budget.

items.label: Breakfast is the best value meal
items.text: A Turkish breakfast spread costs 50-100 lira ($2-4 USD) and includes more variety than lunch or dinner. Eat big at breakfast, lighter later.

items.label: Be cautious of 'special' offers from touts
items.text: Men outside major tourist sites offering 'free' tea or invitations to restaurants are often running commission scams. Politely decline and choose your own spot.

items.label: Visit a supermarket (market, pazar) for snacks
items.text: Turkish bazaars and supermarkets offer cheap, high-quality fresh fruit, cheese, bread, and nuts—better for picnics or budget travelers than tourist restaurants.
